entries.admissibility_relationship: Protocol capability and tool exposure may supply candidate-transition context but do not establish current standing or execution authority.
Agent discovery and task exchange may describe coordination but do not establish delegation validity or commit-time authority.
Validation outcomes may contribute policy or evidence posture but do not independently authorize consequence binding.
Safety classification may inform evidence or policy review but is not equivalent to transition admissibility.
Rail execution may constrain behavior but does not reconstruct action-level standing at commitment.
A policy decision is evidence within a commit-time review and is not itself execution authority.
Authorization evaluation overlaps with commit-time review but does not alone establish continuity, evidence sufficiency, or consequence standing.
Structured control and assessment artifacts may supply evidence references but do not prove implementation truth or authorize execution.
Workload identity and attestation identify an actor or service but do not define its transition-specific authority class.
Credential verification may support identity or evidence posture but does not establish claim truth, current delegation, or execution authority.
Supply-chain layout conformance may support provenance evidence but does not establish runtime admissibility.
Supply-chain assurance may support artifact evidence posture but does not grant deployment or execution authority.
Signature and transparency evidence support integrity and provenance review but do not authorize the signed action.
Authentication and identity claims may identify an actor but do not establish action-level authority or current admissibility.
Token validity and scope may contribute authorization evidence but do not alone establish transition standing at commitment.
Identifier control may support identity and key-binding evidence but does not establish current delegation or execution authority.
Lineage events may improve reconstructability and evidence provenance but do not establish truth, legitimacy, or current authority.
Provenance representation may support reconstruction but does not prove legitimacy, permission, or commit-time standing.

boundary: Terminology reconciliation records preserve external-native vocabulary and classify relationships only. They do not create synonymy, formal equivalence, certification, standing, admissibility, or execution authority.
